\newcommand{\prand}{P_{\mathrm{random}}}

\title{MARC: Verifiable Constraint Solving by Denoising Diffusion over Factor
Graphs, and When Its Learned Proposals Beat Classical Search}
\author{Anonymous submission}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
We present \textbf{MARC}, a neuro-symbolic solver that treats algebraic constraint
problems as \emph{factor graphs} and solves them by denoising diffusion rather than
left-to-right token generation. A graph neural denoiser proposes real-valued variable
assignments by reverse diffusion; a computer-algebra system (CAS) supplies exact
residuals, energies, and gradients as guidance; and a two-stage symbolic checker is
the sole accept gate and the sole training reward---a \emph{derive-not-recall} design
in which no approximate ``solution'' is ever credited. We report three things about
MARC, controls included. (1)~\emph{The learned denoiser converges}: five architectural
fixes take it from diverging (0\% solve) to $1.000$ on convex systems, with a zero
generalization gap. (2)~\emph{Entrapment}: injected noise escapes the deterministic
fixed points that trap gradient descent, cutting entrapment from $1.000$ to $0.475$
(reduction $0.525\pm0.086$, $N{=}200$)---MARC's founding research question, answered
with confidence intervals. (3)~\emph{When do MARC's learned proposals actually beat
classical search?} Adding the controls the amortized-inference literature usually
omits---random multi-start with matched polish, and Levenberg--Marquardt with the
analytic Jacobian---we find and \emph{explain} the answer with a falsifiable law.
MARC's learned proposal wins exactly when the problem's single-start reachability
decays with dimension: for variable-separable families it decays as $v^n$
($\log$-slope $-1.03$), a single measured constant $v{=}0.27$ predicts the whole
random-restart curve with no free parameters (MAE $0.012$), and the learned proposal
holds a flat ceiling while random search collapses; for a coupled chained-bilinear
family reachability stays flat ($\log$-slope $-0.13$), random search never collapses,
the learned proposal ties it, and Levenberg--Marquardt dominates. A real geometry
domain sharpens the diagnostic---it is coupled yet its reachability collapses
($\log$-slope $-0.77$), so the predictor is the measured slope, not a syntactic label.
We report the negative regimes as primary findings: MARC characterizes precisely where
a learned proposal earns its training cost, and where an honest classical baseline
does not.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
Large language models solve mathematics by generating a chain of tokens left to right.
This has three well-known liabilities: benchmark scores can reflect \emph{memorization}
of solutions rather than derivation; verbalized chains need not be \emph{faithful} to
the computation that produced the answer; and mechanical arithmetic consumes model
capacity better spent on structure. MARC is a bet on a different substrate. It
represents a problem not as a token string but as a \textbf{constraint graph}
(variables and symbolic factors), and it \emph{derives} a solution by pushing that
graph toward global consistency under an exact verifier, so that every credited answer
provably satisfies its constraints.

Concretely, MARC is a \emph{neuro-symbolic diffusion solver}. A graph neural network,
trained as a denoiser, proposes real-valued variable assignments by reverse diffusion.
A computer-algebra system computes exact factor residuals, the energy
$E(\mathbf{x})=\sum_j r_j(\mathbf{x})^2$, and its gradient, which both guide the
diffusion and drive a classical energy-descent polish. A two-stage checker (a numeric
gate followed by an exact symbolic/rational check) decides acceptance and is the
\emph{only} source of training reward---no imitation of reference solutions, hence
derive-not-recall. This pairing---neural proposal, classical finisher, exact
checker---is the same division of labor AlphaGeometry uses in geometry-specific
machinery \citep{trinh2024alphageometry}, here on arbitrary algebraic constraint
graphs.

Building MARC surfaces two questions the system is designed to answer, and one that its
own controls forced on us. First, \emph{does noise-driven refinement converge at all}?
We show it does, once five specific conditioning bugs are fixed
(Section~\ref{sec:convergence}). Second, MARC's founding hypothesis: \emph{does injected
noise escape the inconsistent local fixed points that trap deterministic descent}? It
does, measurably (Section~\ref{sec:entrapment}). Third, and this is where an easy story
would have overreached: \emph{does MARC's learned proposal actually beat classical
search}? Only if we compare against the right baseline. The amortized-inference
literature routinely benchmarks a learned-proposal-plus-polish hybrid against
\emph{cold-start} local search, which conflates the value of a diverse restart
distribution with the value of a \emph{learned} one. When we add the honest control
---uniform random multi-start with the identical polish and budget, and a classical
Levenberg--Marquardt solver---MARC's learned advantage becomes conditional, and the
condition turns out to be predictable (Section~\ref{sec:law}).

\paragraph{Contributions.}
\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{MARC}, a neuro-symbolic diffusion solver for algebraic constraint
  systems: factor-graph substrate, CAS-guided graph-denoising diffusion, and an exact
  symbolic checker as the sole accept gate and reward. The learned denoiser converges
  (0\% $\to$ $1.000$, zero generalization gap) after five conditioning fixes we
  document.
\item An \textbf{entrapment} study answering MARC's founding RQ: injected noise reduces
  deterministic trapping by $0.525\pm0.086$ ($N{=}200$, Wilson CIs)---a pre-registered,
  falsifiable result.
\item \textbf{A factorization law} characterizing MARC's learned proposals: a
  falsifiable, parameter-free account of \emph{when} they beat classical search,
  derived from single-start reachability and validated on separable, coupled, and
  real-domain geometry families---with the random-restart and Levenberg--Marquardt
  controls the field usually omits.
\item \textbf{Honest negative regimes as primary findings}: on coupled systems MARC's
  learned proposal ties random restart and loses to Levenberg--Marquardt at every
  dimension---the predicted consequence of a flat reachability curve, reported openly.
\end{enumerate}

\section{The MARC System}
\label{sec:system}
\paragraph{Substrate: constraint graphs.}
A problem is a factor graph with variable nodes $x_i$ and factor nodes carrying
symbolic residual expressions $r_j(\mathbf{x})$ (e.g.\ \texttt{x0+x1-(5)},
\texttt{x0*x1-(6)}). The solution set is $\{\mathbf{x}: r_j(\mathbf{x})=0\ \forall j\}$
and the energy is $E(\mathbf{x})=\sum_j r_j(\mathbf{x})^2$. Procedural generators
produce problems with known solutions and, crucially, allow holding out structural
\emph{patterns} (not just numeric instances) for generalization tests.

\paragraph{CAS in the loop.}
A computer-algebra backend supplies exact $r_j$, $E$, and $\nabla E$ for any graph.
These serve three roles: node features for the denoiser, classifier-style guidance
during reverse diffusion, and the descent direction for the classical polish. Because
residuals are exact and symbolic, MARC never accumulates floating-point drift in the
quantity that defines correctness.

\paragraph{The denoiser.}
A message-passing GNN over the (variable, factor) heterograph, conditioned on the
diffusion time $t$, predicts a denoised assignment. A key architectural finding: a
variable must be conditioned \emph{directly} on the constants of its incident factors
via a zero-initialized skip; without it, message-passing LayerNorm washes constant
magnitudes out and the denoiser cannot place roots (mean $|$err$|$ $5.4\to0.9$ once
added). Training is two-stage: Stage~A denoising-score matching (corruption-and-reverse),
then Stage~B RLVR fine-tuning whose reward is \emph{only} checker acceptance.

\paragraph{The shared polish and the verifier.}
At inference, MARC draws $K$ diffusion proposals and refines each with the same
energy-gradient \texttt{refine} operator (optionally annealed-Langevin), gated by the
same two-stage checker. Every baseline we compare shares this polish and gate and
differs \emph{only} in initialization---deterministic, Langevin, mean-prior, random
multi-start, learned, or Levenberg--Marquardt---so a comparison is a clean test of the
proposal, not of the finisher. This shared-instrument design is what makes the law in
Section~\ref{sec:law} possible.

\section{The Learned Denoiser Converges}
\label{sec:convergence}
The value-diffusion denoiser initially diverged (energy $\sim$$10^4$, 0\% solve). Five
bugs---the denoiser never saw $x_t$; constants were absent from graph tensors; $t$ did
not condition variables; guidance exploded; a one-variable \texttt{squeeze} bug---each
had to be fixed before it learned anything. Fixed, MARC reaches solve rate $1.000$ on
convex linear systems in and out of distribution (generalization gap $0$), with the
Stage-A denoising loss dropping from a flat $\sim$$1.0$ to $\sim$$0.37$. This is an
engineering result, reported as such; it is the precondition for everything below.

\section{Entrapment: Noise Escapes Deterministic Traps}
\label{sec:entrapment}
MARC's founding hypothesis (RQ2) is that stochasticity in the refinement escapes the
locally-consistent-but-globally-wrong fixed points that trap deterministic descent. On
200 non-convex problems whose starts sit inside a spurious basin behind an energy
barrier, deterministic energy descent is \textbf{$100\%$ trapped}; annealed-noise
(Langevin) descent cuts entrapment to $0.475$, a reduction of
$\mathbf{0.525\pm0.086}$ (95\% CI excludes $0$; $N{=}200$, 5 seeds). The mechanism is
textbook annealed Langevin---we claim the pre-registered, CI-backed \emph{measurement},
not a new sampler---and it matters for the next section, because it says stochastic
\emph{search} already escapes traps without any learned proposal.

\section{When Do MARC's Learned Proposals Beat Classical Search?}
\label{sec:law}
Section~\ref{sec:entrapment} raises the sharp question: if stochastic search already
escapes traps, what does the \emph{learned} proposal add over simply restarting search
many times? We answer with a law, using the shared-instrument design of
Section~\ref{sec:system}.

\paragraph{The atom: single-start reachability.}
Define $q(n)=\Pr_{x_0\sim U}[\text{polish}(x_0)\ \text{accepted}]$, the probability
that one random start, pushed through MARC's shared polish, lands in an accepting
basin. Best-of-$K$ random restart is then exactly
\begin{equation}
\prand(n;K)=1-\big(1-q(n)\big)^K. \label{eq:bok}
\end{equation}
A hybrid that merely beats cold-start Langevin has proven nothing about learning: it
must beat \eqref{eq:bok} at the same $K$.

\paragraph{The dichotomy, and the real diagnostic.}
How $q(n)$ scales decides everything. When factors are variable-\emph{separable}
(each touches one variable) MARC's polish is coordinate-decoupled, so a start is
accepted iff every coordinate independently lands in its basin:
\begin{equation}
q_{\mathrm{sep}}(n)=v^{\,n},\qquad v:=q(1). \label{eq:vn}
\end{equation}
Then $\log q$ is linear with slope $\log v<0$: reachability decays geometrically,
random search needs $\Theta(v^{-n})$ starts and collapses, while a learned proposal
that reproduces each marginal stays flat and must eventually win. Coupled families need
not obey \eqref{eq:vn}; whether they collapse is empirical. The \emph{measured} $\log q$
slope is therefore the diagnostic: steep (collapse) $\Rightarrow$ learning can help;
flat $\Rightarrow$ it cannot. With a flat learned ceiling $p_L$, learning overtakes
search at $n^\*=\lceil \log(1-(1-p_L)^{1/K})/\log v\rceil$, finite iff $v<1$.

\paragraph{Validation (parameter-free).}
Using MARC's identical generators, polish, and checker, we measure $q(n)$ over $600$
fresh instances per $n$ (Wilson CIs; Table~\ref{tab:law}, Figure~\ref{fig:law}).
\emph{Separable:} slope $-1.03$ ($R^2{=}0.98$); with only $v{=}0.27$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:vn}
into \eqref{eq:bok} reproduces the measured random-restart curve
($0.90,0.47,0.16,0.03,0.00$) to \textbf{MAE $0.012$}; the expected restart budget
$1/q(n)$ explodes $3.7{\to}600$. MARC's learned proposal holds a flat $0.95$ ceiling
across $n{=}1$--$4$ while random collapses---the gap widens monotonically.
\emph{Coupled bilinear:} slope $-0.13$ ($R^2{=}0.96$), budget flat $2.0{\to}4.3$;
random never collapses, MARC's learned proposal ties it at every dimension, and
Levenberg--Marquardt saturates (Table~\ref{tab:coupled}). \emph{Geometry (real
domain):} chains of unknown points with squared-distance constraints---syntactically
coupled, yet reachability \emph{collapses} (slope $-0.77$, $R^2{=}0.999$) because each
point's two-circle subproblem has a reflection ambiguity and spurious basins, so errors
compound. The law thus flags geometry as a real domain where a learned proposal
\emph{should} help (classical-polish solve rates there are non-saturated:
in-distribution $0.56$, held-out $0.28$)---MARC's concrete next target.

\begin{table}[t]\centering\small
\caption{The factorization law across three families: the measured single-start
reachability slope predicts the regime. Source: \texttt{crossover\_theory.json}.}
\label{tab:law}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
 & Separable & Coupled bilin. & Geometry (real) \\
\midrule
$\log q(n)$ slope $b$ & $-1.03$ & $-0.13$ & $-0.77$ \\
fit $R^2$ & $0.98$ & $0.96$ & $0.999$ \\
$E[\text{starts}]{=}1/q$ & $3.7{\to}600$ & $2.0{\to}4.3$ & collapses \\
random search & collapses & survives & collapses \\
learned helps? & \textbf{yes} (R5) & \textbf{no} (R7) & predicted yes \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[t]\centering\small

random; Levenberg--Marquardt dominates---the predicted flat-reachability regime.}
\label{tab:coupled}
\begin{tabular}{lccccc}
\toprule
$n$ & Langevin & random & LM & learned & $p(\mathrm{l}{>}\mathrm{rand})$\\
\midrule
2 & .17 & .48 & .77 & .23 & ns\\

\section{Related Work}
\paragraph{Amortized and learned proposals for optimization.}
Diffusion and generative solvers for combinatorial optimization
\citep{sun2023difusco,unsupco2024}, GFlowNet samplers \citep{bengio2021gflownet}, and
amortized-inference / bootstrapped-refinement hybrids
\citep{taniguchi2022amortized,bootstrapdiffusion2025}, built on denoising diffusion
\citep{ho2020ddpm}, pair a learned proposal with local refinement. MARC differs in
substrate (continuous \emph{algebraic} constraint graphs with a symbolic CAS and an
exact checker in the loop) and in method: we ask what the learned proposal contributes
\emph{once a random-restart control of equal budget is present}, and give the
measurable condition under which the answer is positive.

\paragraph{Neural proposals with symbolic checking.}
AlphaGeometry \citep{trinh2024alphageometry} pairs neural proposals with a symbolic
engine in geometry-specific machinery. MARC generalizes the neural-proposal / classical
-finisher / exact-checker pattern to arbitrary algebraic constraint graphs and uses it
to \emph{characterize} when the neural component helps.

\paragraph{Classical constraint solving.}
Multi-start Levenberg--Marquardt with analytic Jacobians \citep{nocedal2006numerical}
is the strong baseline for polynomial systems; we report it explicitly. Langevin
stochastic search \citep{reglangevin2025} is our cold-start baseline and the basis of
the entrapment analysis.

\section{Limitations}
MARC targets continuous algebraic constraint systems; it is not a general
mathematical-reasoning engine (a template formalizer covers $0/48$ of a MATH sample---an
autoformalization bottleneck, not a solver result). All families are
synthetic-but-checkable; the real-domain geometry result is a reachability measurement,
and training MARC's denoiser on geometry to confirm the predicted learned gain is the
flagged next step. Eq.~\eqref{eq:bok} uses instance-averaged $q$, so we validate against
the self-measured best-of-$K$ curve under identical conditions. We do not claim MARC's
learned solver beats classical search in general---the coupled result is the opposite;
the contribution is the system plus a law that says precisely \emph{when} it does.

\section{Conclusion}
MARC solves algebraic constraint problems by denoising diffusion over factor graphs
under an exact symbolic checker---a verifiable, derive-not-recall alternative to
token-level reasoning. Its learned denoiser converges, its injected noise escapes
deterministic traps, and---the result we most want reviewers to take away---whether its
learned proposals beat classical search is not a matter of taste in baselines but of a
measurable property: single-start reachability. When reachability collapses with
dimension a learned proposal must win; when it stays flat, honest classical search
wins, and MARC says so. That characterization, controls and negatives included, is what
a learned constraint solver should be held to.
